segments.title: Assessment Appraisal Advocacy and Item Holds Including Parkside Commons
segments.summary: Council member Williams presents items 24-32; item 24 held, item 26 (property appraisal) debated. Commissioner Michael Collins (Neighborhood and Business Development) advocates moving forward with appraisal despite council hesitation, noting it does not commit to purchase and assessor backlog could delay the project. Council and deputy mayor agree to discuss offline. Remainder of items ready.

segments.title: Parkside Commons Community Meeting Requirements, Flock Camera Hold, and Vape Recycling Inquiry
segments.summary: Multiple items covered: (1) Parkside Commons (item 43) held — council requests developer schedule at least three more community meetings; (2) Item 44 sent back to planning commission; (3) Flock camera ordinance (item 45) held pending Axon contract resolution; (4) Chief Russen confirms DWI item (46) ready; (5) CRB reappointment (48) ready. Additionally, Council member Caldwell inquires about vape disposal — OCRRA director of recycling explains vapes contain batteries and pharmaceuticals requiring separate management; state advisory committee developing a program. Public hearing on battery moratorium scheduled for that evening.

political_signal.description: Council requires additional community engagement for Parkside Commons development and holds Flock cameras pending Axon contract review

processing_notes: Flock camera ordinance held pending Axon contract connects to b75K4ejDfY4 and -26j2mt6jMU seg 19
Vape recycling is separate from battery kiosk program due to pharmaceutical content
Parkside Commons HDFC is a housing development fund corporation — significant community engagement concerns
Williams also presents item 49 (board of assessment review reappointment)

segments.title: Fire Apparatus Replacement Follow-Up: Training Truck Status and Fleet Deficiencies
segments.summary: Council member Dr. Chol Majok follows up on fire apparatus replacement data requested at Jan 21 study session. Training truck #1 has 86,000 miles, is severely overdue, and can no longer be used on roads — limited to recruit training for understanding truck components. Three spare trucks also past their operational life by one to five years. Current legislation (items 20-21) would replace one of the end-of-life trucks.

political_signal.description: Council follows up on detailed apparatus replacement data, questioning severity of fleet deficiencies

processing_notes: Continues fire apparatus discussion from Jan 21 study session (-26j2mt6jMU seg 8)
Training truck #1 is past road-legal life but retained for recruit instruction

segments.title: Fire Apparatus Replacement Program: Decades of Deferred Maintenance Nearly Resolved
segments.summary: Fire Chief explains apparatus replacement history — decades of deferred maintenance created a 14-truck deficit across engines and ladders. Over the last 8 years, systematic purchasing has nearly closed the gap. Current request (items 20-21) for one ladder truck, plus planned two-engine purchase next year, will bring the program to full compliance with no further deficiencies. End-of-life trucks auctioned. Meeting adjourns after this discussion.

political_signal.description: Administration investment resolving long-standing fire apparatus infrastructure deficit through systematic replacement program

processing_notes: 14-truck deficit accumulated over decades of neglected replacement program
8-year catch-up effort nearly complete — one more ladder + two engines will achieve full compliance
Connects to Jan 21 study session fire apparatus discussion (-26j2mt6jMU seg 8)
